text: The estimated number of uranium processes developed to extract uranium from wet phosphoric acid (WPA) over the 40 years from 1951 to 1991 has been estimated as seventeen. These processes were sensitive to the uranium market and following fluctuations were almost without exception backed down whenever uranium prices fell. All WPA uranium extraction plants were shutdown by the end of the 1990's. However, with an increasing world uranium requirement, anticipated growth of the nuclear power program in emerging countries and a re-evaluation of existing technology, a review of potential applications of historic solutions, in addition to possible improvements, with the possible development of new and alternative technologies to extract uranium efficiently and selectively in present day WPA hydrometallurgy conditions is pertinent. This article makes a retrospective evaluation of the various historic extraction processes together with the associated underlying physicochemistry, reviews present hydrometallurgical developments including recent studies on new extraction process developments applicable in present day phosphoric acid containing uranium solutions.
explanation: The text is coherent, domain-specific, and reads like an academic abstract; it could be human-written, but the measured, slightly repetitive phrasing is also typical of AI-generated drafts.
